lesson.menu_title: Negotiation VII -- The Full Deal & Lessons Learned
lesson.menu_desc: Run the full deal cycle from first proposal to signed renewal, then reflect on the turning points -- imagine a different past with the third conditional (if we had not protected the goodwill, we would not have renewed) -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Negotiation VII -- the full deal simulation and lessons learned, with the third conditional
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Nilo Mesquita Patussi | Aula 20 -- Negotiation VII

listenings.text: Hello again, Doctor Nilo, Sofia here from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. This is a special class, your twentieth, the end of the negotiation block. Look how far you have come. You have learned to open a negotiation, make soft proposals, close a deal, read and sign a contract, manage the relationship, run a renewal meeting, and review a whole partnership. Today you put it all together, and you reflect on the lessons learned. So here is some advice. First, the words for reflection. When you finally win something difficult, you overcome it. A setback is a problem that pushes you back for a while. A breakthrough is the moment a big problem suddenly opens up. A turning point is the exact moment when everything changed direction. When you say in hindsight, or on reflection, you mean now, looking back, you understand it better. A decision can be strategic, part of a bigger plan, decisive, the one that settles the matter, or pivotal, the one that everything turned on. A landmark deal is so important that people remember it. And the legacy is what the deal leaves behind for the future. Second, the grammar for reflection, the third conditional. When you imagine a different past, a past that did not happen, you use if plus the past perfect, and would have plus the past participle. You say, if we had not protected the goodwill, we would not have renewed the deal. If I had signed the first draft, we would have lost the exclusivity. If we had gone to court, we would have destroyed the partnership. This grammar is perfect for lessons learned, because it shows the value of the right decision by imagining the wrong one. Third, the big picture. Look back at the whole journey, name the turning points, and use the third conditional to show why each decision mattered. All in all, Doctor, you have built a complete negotiator. Run the full deal one last time, reflect with hindsight, and carry these lessons into every contract you sign. It has been a pleasure. Good luck, and congratulations.

Let me look back at the whole story of our biggest deal at Corinthians, from the first proposal to the long-term renewal. It is the best example of everything I have learned. At the start, we opened the negotiation politely and made soft proposals. The first turning point came when the sponsor pushed back hard on the price. If I had accepted their first offer, we would have lost a lot of money. But I held my deal breaker, and we found a compromise. Then came the contract. I read every clause, and I found a dangerous one about global logo rights. In hindsight, that was the most decisive moment. If I had signed the first draft, we would have given away the exclusivity in Brazil. So I asked them to amend it. The hardest setback came in the second season, when the sponsor paid late. If we had gone straight to court, we would have destroyed the goodwill and lost the partnership forever. Instead, I sent a polite notice, offered a remedy, and we renegotiated. That was the pivotal decision. Because we protected the relationship, the breakthrough came at the end of the season, the sponsor wanted to renew. We ran a formal meeting, passed the resolution, and ratified a long-term deal. On reflection, the whole thing turned on one strategic idea, after you sign, you are not a winner and a loser, you are two partners. If we had not built that trust, none of this would have happened. It became a landmark deal for the club, and its legacy is a partnership that is still growing. All in all, that is the biggest lesson of the negotiation block. A great deal is not about winning once. It is about the decisions that protect the relationship, season after season.
